name: FleetSync
title: Operator-Side Frac Sand Logistics Command Center
oneLiner: Gantt scheduling, daily truck-allocation math, and scenario planning for the completions team coordinating sand across multiple frac fleets — the operator-side planning layer that today lives in Excel because every incumbent's software is welded to their hardware.

tags.target: Completions & sand-logistics coordinators at shale operators and pressure pumpers
tags.mainCompetitor: Excel (real incumbent); PropX PropConnect / Solaris / Atlas (hardware-tied)

opportunity.note: Budgets are real (oilfield software prices in $1K+/fleet/mo territory) but the buyer set is narrow — operators and pressure pumpers — and incumbents bundle 'free' software with sand, boxes, and trucking. The open slot is the hardware-agnostic operator-side planning layer.

problemSeverity.note: A frac crew consumes a truckload of sand roughly every 10 minutes, 24/7 (~144 loads/day/crew); last-mile logistics is up to one-third of delivered sand cost; waiting-on-sand downtime runs ~$5,500-$10,000+ per hour. Mis-planned allocation is one of the most expensive spreadsheet errors in shale.

feasibility.note: Built and functional: ~20.3K LOC across 8 pages (Gantt, allocation grid, scenarios, workload intelligence), shaped by daily use inside a real completions program. Docked from 9: zero tests, two live type errors in core pages, and dead scaffolding — the quality foundation lags the feature set.

whyNow.note: The 2026 'mega-LNG wave' (Corpus Christi Stage 3, Plaquemines Phase 2 online) has EIA projecting record US gas output in 2026-27; Appalachia alone needs ~50 more rigs and frac crews for the next 35 Bcf/d, with ~824 DUCs queued. More crews × higher sand intensity (~2,500 lb/lateral-ft) = more coordination pain per operator.

revenuePotential.note: Per-fleet or per-program SaaS at $1K-$5K/mo (est.) is credible for software that visibly prevents $10K/hr downtime; a handful of operator customers is a real business. Gated today by IP/employment questions, not by the market.

executionDifficulty.note: The remaining technical work is ordinary (tests, auth, audit trail, analytics). The genuinely hard part is non-technical: IP cleanliness for a tool born inside an employer's operations, and selling into that employer's industry while employed there.

goToMarket.note: As a personal product today, GTM is blocked pending IP/conflict clarity — the natural first customers are the builder's employer's orbit. Post-clarity, the path is strong: an operator-proven tool sold peer-to-peer through completions networks is exactly how oilfield software spreads.

businessFit.founderFit: As high as founder-market fit gets — the tool was built by the person doing the job it automates, inside a live completions program, and every feature (lane cascading, NPT sub-categories, sandbox scenarios) encodes real operational scar tissue. The same origin is the constraint: employment and IP position must be resolved in writing before this becomes a product.

summary.problem: A modern frac crew burns a truckload of sand every ~10 minutes around the clock; last-mile logistics is as much as a third of delivered sand cost, and a crew waiting on sand costs $5,500-$10,000+ per hour. The software incumbents all sell hardware or services first — Solaris silos, PropX containers with PropConnect, Atlas trucking with Opti dispatch — so their tools optimize their own asset utilization and dispatch, not the operator's cross-fleet plan. The operator-side questions — which pads run on which fleet lanes, how many trucks per hauler per day, what breaks if a pad slips a week, baseline vs. forecast vs. actual — live in Excel, rebuilt by every completions team, breaking silently under simul-frac pace.
summary.solution: FleetSync is the operator-side sand logistics command center: an interactive Gantt of frac jobs by fleet lane with drag-and-drop rescheduling and lane cascading, an Excel-like daily truck-allocation grid with hauler totals / frac needs / surplus rows and real-time conflict detection, truck-demand math from stage pace (stages/day × tons/stage → loads/day with storage-type overrides), scenario management (baseline / forecast / actual / sandbox with isolation), an NPT journal with sub-categorized causes, and CSV/Excel import-export both ways. Hardware-agnostic by design — it doesn't care whose boxes, silos, or trucks are on location.
summary.wedge: The sand-logistics coordinator at a gas operator running multiple frac fleets — a planning buyer, not a dispatch buyer. Win the plan (the Gantt + allocation grid that replaces the sacred spreadsheet), and expansion into actuals, analytics, and hauler-facing views follows. Every incumbent must sell hardware to justify their software; this sells the plan itself.

whyNow.narrative: Gas completions activity is entering a multi-year ramp: the 2026 LNG wave (Corpus Christi Stage 3 and Plaquemines Phase 2 both due online) has the EIA projecting record US natural gas production in 2026 and 2027, and Appalachia alone is estimated to need ~50 additional rigs and frac crews to deliver the next 35 Bcf/d, with ~824 drilled-but-uncompleted wells queued. Meanwhile sand intensity keeps climbing (~2,500 lb per lateral foot is now the sweet spot in core Marcellus) and simul-frac/e-frac pace compresses the margin for logistics error. The incumbents consolidated around hardware (Liberty acquired PropX; Atlas built dispatch around its own trucking), leaving the operator-side planning layer unclaimed. And the build cost collapsed: this app itself was AI-assisted into existence by a practitioner on the job.
drivers: LNG pull: Corpus Christi Stage 3 and Plaquemines Phase 2 liquefaction online in 2026; ~80% growth in US LNG export capacity projected over five years.
EIA projects record US natural gas production in 2026 and 2027 — completions activity follows.
Appalachia: ~824 DUCs and an estimated ~50 additional rigs/frac crews needed for 35 Bcf/d of new production — each new crew is another coordination surface.
Sand intensity: ~2,500 lb/lateral-ft designs and simul-frac operations multiply daily tonnage and truck cadence per pad.
Incumbent structure: last-mile software is bundled with proppant, containers, or trucking (Solaris, PropX/Liberty, Atlas, Vorto) — none is incented to sell the operator a neutral planning tool.
AI-assisted development lets a practitioner build the internal tool their vendor never would — FleetSync is the existence proof.

searchDemand.note: B2B niche — demand shows up in vendor marketing (PropConnect, Atlas Opti) and procurement conversations, not consumer search volume.
A persistent genre of industry analysis (AOGR, PLG Consulting) quantifying last-mile share of delivered cost — the pain is measured and re-measured, which means budgets exist.

communities.name: Petroleum Connection
communities.signal: Organic threads asking for exactly this category ('good ticketing/inventory software for frac sand operations') — unprompted demand for tooling in the niche.

communities.name: Completions / frac sand professional networks
communities.signal: The actual buyer watering hole: completions managers, sand coordinators, and logistics leads are dense and reachable via peer content — the standard oilfield-software GTM channel.

communities.name: AOGR / Hart Energy completions coverage
communities.signal: Last-mile logistics gets recurring cover-story treatment — vendor-neutral evidence the category is a recognized cost center.

marketStats.stat: Last-mile logistics comprises as much as one-third of the total delivered cost of frac sand; for Northern White sand, logistics can reach ~75% of delivered cost.
A crew waiting on sand incurs ~$5,500/hr on smaller operations and $10,000+/hr on larger ones; carrier wait time adds ~$2,000/hr.
A modern frac crew requires a truckload of sand roughly every 10 minutes, 24/7 — on the order of 144 loads/day.
EIA projects US natural gas production to reach record highs in 2026 and 2027 on LNG export growth.
Appalachia holds ~824 DUC wells (second-largest US inventory) and needs an estimated ~50 additional rigs and frac crews to deliver 35 Bcf/d of new production.
Core Marcellus completion designs center on ~2,500 lb of proppant per lateral foot at 200-ft stage spacing.

marketGap.narrative: Every serious software player in frac sand logistics is a hardware or services company first: their software exists to sell and utilize their assets, and it optimizes their slice — container cycling, silo inventory, truck dispatch. The operator's cross-fleet planning problem (multi-pad Gantt, hauler allocation, what-if scenarios, baseline-vs-actual) is nobody's product, because a neutral planning tool doesn't move sand tonnage or truck hours. So it defaults to Excel — unversioned, single-user, formula-fragile — at exactly the moment simul-frac pace and LNG-driven activity make the plan both more complex and more expensive to get wrong. The gap is the neutral, operator-owned planning layer; FleetSync is that layer, already shaped by production use inside a shale gas operator's completions program.

competitors.name: PropX (PropConnect) — Liberty Energy
competitors.positioning: Last-mile container/wet-sand logistics with the PropConnect platform spanning operators, dispatchers, and trucking.
competitors.weakness: Software rides the hardware: it exists where PropX equipment is on location, and it optimizes PropX's logistics loop — not the operator's cross-fleet, multi-vendor plan.

competitors.name: Solaris (incl. former SandBox)
competitors.positioning: Silo and container systems with inventory/telemetry software; major Permian share.
competitors.weakness: Same structure: the software is an attachment to sand storage hardware. No neutral planning/scenario layer for pads served by other vendors.

competitors.name: Atlas Energy Solutions (Opti Ordering / Opti Dispatch)
competitors.positioning: Vertically integrated Permian sand + trucking with autonomous ordering/dispatch.
competitors.weakness: Brilliant at dispatching Atlas sand on Atlas trucks in one basin; structurally incapable of being the operator's vendor-neutral planning system, and Appalachia isn't its ground.

competitors.name: Vorto / Automatize-class dispatch platforms
competitors.positioning: AI dispatch and carrier marketplaces for bulk oilfield logistics.
competitors.weakness: Dispatch-time optimization, not plan-time: they route today's loads; they don't hold the 6-month Gantt, the allocation grid, or the sandbox scenario.

competitors.name: Excel + shared drives (the real incumbent)
competitors.positioning: Every completions team's home-grown workbook: lanes as tabs, allocations as cells, tribal formulas.
competitors.weakness: Single-user, unversioned, no conflict detection, breaks silently when a pad slips — and the person who built the workbook eventually changes jobs.

mvp: Already built (evidence pass, 2026-07-18): full CRUD for frac jobs/haulers/lanes/scenarios/schedules/allocations; drag-and-drop Gantt with lane cascading; Excel-like allocation grid with bulk edit, drag-to-fill, and three summary footer rows; real-time conflict detection; scenario cloning with sandbox isolation; truck-demand breakdowns with overrides; NPT journal; CSV/XLSX import and export; role-based planner/viewer access.

Gate 0a (data hygiene): DONE 2026-07-18 — repo private, history republished clean, verified. Gate 0b (written IP/ownership and employment-conflict clarity) still blocks any product motion — see fit report.
Quality floor: delete dead scaffolding, fix the two live type errors, stand up a test harness with the truck-demand engine covered first (it's the money math).
Replace env-var usernames with real authentication and per-org tenancy — the difference between an internal tool and a sellable product.
Audit trail + change history (already on the roadmap) — table stakes for multi-user planning trust.
firstCustomers: None until Gate 0 clears — deliberately. The natural first customers sit in or near the builder's employer's orbit, which is exactly why the IP/conflict question gates everything.

Post-gate: one friendly mid-size Appalachian or Haynesville operator running 2+ fleets, recruited peer-to-peer through completions networks, free pilot instrumented on planning-time saved and allocation conflicts caught.
Pressure pumpers' logistics teams as a second segment — they feel the same allocation pain from the service side.
Trade-press credibility loop: the last-mile cost genre (AOGR et al.) is hungry for operator-side tooling stories.

pricing.model: Per-fleet (lane) subscription, operator-level contract; all numbers speculative (est.) pending Gate 0 and first pilots.

tiers.name: Pilot
tiers.includes: One fleet lane, full planning stack, success metrics instrumented.

tiers.name: Program
tiers.includes: All lanes, scenarios, conflict detection, import/export, audit trail.

tiers.name: Enterprise
tiers.includes: SSO, hauler-facing views, analytics dashboards, priority support.

milestones90Days: Weeks 1-2: Gate 0b — the IP/conflict memo (Gate 0a, repo containment, closed 2026-07-18). Nothing customer-facing matters until this is done.
Weeks 3-6: Quality floor — dead code deleted, type-clean, test harness with demand-engine coverage, CI green gate.
Weeks 7-10: Real auth + tenancy; audit trail.

Weeks 11-12: Re-run /idea-fit. If Gate 0 resolved favorably, this verdict upgrades and the pilot search starts; if not, FleetSync remains a work-machine internal tool and a portfolio proof, which is also a fine outcome.

valueLadder.offer: Truck-demand calculator (stages/day × tons/stage → loads/day, storage-type aware) as a free web tool
valueLadder.goal: Demonstrate the core math to completions coordinators; harvest the buyer conversation. (Speculative — post-Gate 0.)
Land inside the workflow that replaces the sacred spreadsheet; measure conflicts caught and planning hours saved.

valueLadder.offer: Program subscription — full planning stack, all lanes
valueLadder.goal: The operating contract; priced against $10K/hr downtime and a third-of-delivered-cost logistics line.

valueLadder.offer: Analytics + actuals: NPT rollups, plan-vs-actual variance, hauler scorecards
valueLadder.goal: Turn accumulated planning data into the retention moat — the longer it runs, the smarter the plan.

valueLadder.offer: Enterprise: SSO, hauler portals, multi-program rollups
valueLadder.goal: Expand from one completions team to the operator's whole program.

valueEquation.dreamOutcome: No crew ever waits on sand because the plan saw the conflict a week early; the coordinator runs scenarios in minutes instead of rebuilding a workbook at 5 a.m.
valueEquation.perceivedLikelihood: High with the origin story told: built inside a live completions program by the person doing the job — the rarest credibility in oilfield software.
valueEquation.timeDelay: Days: import the current sand plan (CSV/XLSX already supported), see conflicts immediately.
valueEquation.effortAndSacrifice: The real cost is switching off the sacred spreadsheet — mitigated by the grid literally behaving like Excel (inline edit, drag-to-fill, bulk ops).
valueEquation.verdict: A strong offer for a measured, expensive pain — once the non-market gates (IP, exposure) clear. The equation is currently blocked on likelihood-of-being-sellable, not on customer value.

marketMatrix.explanation: High-uniqueness insight (the hardware-neutral, operator-owned planning layer) into a deliberately narrow market (completions teams at shale operators). The ceiling is a category-defining vertical tool, not a horizontal platform — and at oilfield price points, that ceiling is a real business.

acp.audience: Completions managers and sand-logistics coordinators at gas-weighted operators and pressure pumpers — a small, dense, name-knowable population.
acp.community: Not consumer-style communities: LinkedIn completions networks, SPE, trade press, and peer referral — the standard oilfield-software channel, where one credible operator logo unlocks the next three.
acp.product: A planning tool that behaves like the spreadsheet it replaces, carries the domain's exact vocabulary (lanes, stages, NPT sub-causes, storage types), and stays neutral across sand/box/trucking vendors.

risks.risk: IP/ownership ambiguity: the tool originated inside an employer's operations; the employer may have claims on it, and selling in the employer's industry while employed is a conflict minefield.
risks.mitigation: Written clarity before any product motion — employment agreement review, possibly a formal carve-out. This is Gate 0 alongside exposure remediation; the 90-day plan does nothing customer-facing before it.

risks.risk: Repo data exposure (RESOLVED 2026-07-18): real operational data from the origin deployment had survived in the public repo's tree and full history despite an earlier tip-only scrub.
risks.mitigation: Done — repo made private (zero forks/stars), contaminated files removed, docs genericized, history republished from a fresh root, verified clean via full-history sweep of a fresh clone. Standing rule: exports and scratch assets are gitignored; re-run the sweep before any future re-publication.

risks.risk: Quality foundation lags the feature set: zero tests, live type errors, dead scaffolding — risky ground for a tool whose output allocates $10K/hr resources.
risks.mitigation: Test harness starting with the truck-demand engine, type-clean gate in CI, delete the boilerplate.

risks.risk: Incumbent response: PropX/Atlas could ship operator-neutral planning views if the niche proves out.
risks.mitigation: Their business models resist neutrality (software exists to move their assets); speed plus operator-side trust is the moat, and Appalachia is under-served by Permian-centric incumbents.

risks.risk: Single-buyer-type concentration in a cyclical industry: gas activity downturns freeze oilfield software budgets.
risks.mitigation: Price per fleet (scales down gracefully), keep the tool valuable at planning time even when activity slows — scenario planning matters most when schedules are in flux.

sources.title: AOGR — Last-mile solutions and delivered-cost share of frac sand logistics
LogistixIQ — Frac sand delivery cadence and waiting-on-sand NPT costs
PLG Consulting — Frac sand transportation & logistics economics
PropX — Last-mile logistics and the PropConnect platform
Atlas Energy Solutions — Opti Ordering / Opti Dispatch last-mile platform
EIA — US natural gas production to reach record highs in 2026 and 2027
AOGR — Appalachian producers, LNG demand, rigs/crews needed, sand intensity
Petroleum Connection — organic demand thread for frac sand ops software

fitReport.projectPath: github.com/mschneider2185/FleetSync (fresh clone; no local working copy — the origin deployment was migrated to a separate work machine)
fitReport.auditRef: No formal /project-audit exists. Grounded in a scoped evidence pass (2026-07-18): fresh clone of the public GitHub repo, README-vs-code review, TypeScript check, test/LOC census, 215-commit history review, and a data-scrub verification sweep. A full /project-audit is recommended before any product motion.
fitReport.verdictNote: Park the public product ambition — not the product. Gate 0a (data hygiene) CLOSED 2026-07-18: repo set private (zero forks/stars, so nothing was copied out), contaminated files removed, history republished from a fresh root, verified clean by a full-history sweep of a fresh clone. Gate 0b remains OPEN: the tool was born inside an employer's operations, so IP/ownership and employment-conflict clarity must exist in writing before a single customer conversation — that alone holds the verdict at PARK. If 0b resolves favorably, the market timing (2026-27 LNG-driven completions ramp) and the open operator-side planning slot likely upgrade this to BUILD ON at the scheduled re-run. The internal version continuing on the work machine is unaffected by this verdict.
fitReport.wedgeCheck: The product nails the researched wedge with unusual precision — it was built by the wedge persona (a sand-logistics coordinator) inside a live completions program, and the feature set encodes the job's real edge cases (lane cascading, NPT sub-causes, sandbox scenario isolation, storage-type demand overrides). There is no product drift. The drift risk is positional, not product: the personal/public fork currently has no servable customer until the IP and exposure gates clear, and pretending otherwise would aim a good product at a legal wall.

completion.aspect: Planning Gantt with drag-and-drop, lane cascading, zoom levels
completion.evidence: README verified against client/src/pages/dashboard.tsx and workload.tsx; commit history shows iterative refinement through 2026-07

completion.aspect: Excel-like daily allocation grid (inline/bulk edit, drag-to-fill, summary footers)
completion.evidence: allocation-grid.tsx; README feature list matches implementation
Scenario CRUD + isolation in schema and storage layer — the differentiator no incumbent planning surface has
README verified; dismissible warnings wired into planning views

completion.aspect: Truck-demand engine (stage pace → loads/day with overrides)
completion.evidence: Demand breakdowns with date-segmented overrides; the core money-math of the product — currently has zero test coverage
Six NPT sub-categories; Gantt shows journal-day indicators; a warehouse-sync integration existed in the origin era
Import for sand plans, export for grid + per-frac reports; server/import + sand-actuals modules
0 test files; no test script in package.json; 15 tsc errors (13 in dead Replit boilerplate, 2 live in workload.tsx); no CI
Access control is a PLANNER_USERNAMES env var — an internal-tool pattern, not a product one
README 'What's next'; required for multi-user planning trust
README 'What's next'; the retention rung of the value ladder
README 'What's next'; field users live on phones and tablets

Remediated 2026-07-18: repo private, contaminated export + scratch-asset folder removed, doc references genericized, seed data confirmed fictional, history republished from a fresh root (215 commits → 1); verified by a full-history sweep of a fresh clone. Prior history retained in a local mirror backup only

scopeRulings.observation: Replit scaffolding (chat/image integrations, boilerplate package name 'rest-express') rode along from the app's origin and accounts for 13 of 15 type errors.
scopeRulings.ruling: Cut — delete server/replit_integrations and rename the package; it's dead weight distorting the health signal.

scopeRulings.observation: A real planning-data export file sits in the repository tree.
scopeRulings.ruling: Cut immediately as part of Gate 0 containment; exports belong in gitignored local space (the FEV convention).

scopeRulings.observation: Landing page and public-product framing exist while the gates are unresolved.
scopeRulings.ruling: Defer — no public-facing product surface until Gate 0 clears; the landing page becomes useful the day the pilot search starts.

scopeRulings.observation: Origin-era integration code (warehouse sync, actuals matching) encodes the previous deployment's environment.
scopeRulings.ruling: Keep but generalize behind adapters before any pilot — it's evidence of depth, but it must not assume one company's data shapes.

fitReport.monetization: Zero rungs in code (no auth beyond an env var, no billing, no tenancy) — appropriate, since selling is gated anyway. The research says the eventual model is per-fleet SaaS priced against $10K/hr downtime, with a free instrumented pilot as the wedge. Build nothing monetization-shaped until Gate 0 clears; build the quality floor instead, because a tool allocating $10K/hr resources will be judged on trustworthiness first.

scoreAdjustments.reason: Research alone would guess 6 for 'niche vertical planning SaaS'; the evidence shows ~20.3K LOC of working, production-shaped product. Held below 8 by the missing quality floor: zero tests, live type errors, no CI.

The evidence pass surfaced what market research never would: the natural customer base sits in the builder's employer's orbit, and the public repo exposes origin-deployment data — GTM is gated on legal/compliance clearance, not channel access.
The 2026-27 gas completions ramp (LNG wave, record EIA projections, Appalachian crew additions) is stronger than a generic 'oilfield digitization' thesis — the window is real and near-term.

prd.problem: A working operator-side sand logistics planner has outgrown its internal-tool origins but is blocked from becoming a product by two gates (public-repo data exposure; unresolved IP/employment position) and from being trustworthy by a missing quality floor (no tests, live type errors, env-var auth).
prd.targetUser: Today: the builder, as maintainer of a portfolio-grade asset. Post-Gate-0: completions and sand-logistics coordinators at gas-weighted operators running 2+ frac fleets.
coreFeatures: Gate 0a — containment: repository private, then a history rewrite removing the planning export, origin-era seeds, and attached internal documents; verified by a full-history sweep
Gate 0b — a written IP/conflict position (employment agreement review; carve-out if needed) before any customer-facing step
Delete server/replit_integrations and fix the two live type errors in workload.tsx; rename the package from its boilerplate name
Test harness (Vitest) with the truck-demand engine covered first, then allocation-total and lane-cascade math; CI gate on test + typecheck
Real authentication and per-organization tenancy replacing the PLANNER_USERNAMES env var
Audit trail: who changed which allocation/schedule when — the trust feature multi-user planning requires
Analytics v1: plan-vs-actual variance and NPT rollups from data already captured
nonGoals: No customer conversations, pilots, landing-page promotion, or pricing experiments until both Gate 0 items are resolved in writing
No dispatch/telematics features — plan-time is the wedge; dispatch is the incumbents' fortified ground
No basin-specific integrations that assume any one company's data shapes
No mobile apps yet — responsive web after the quality floor
successMetrics: Gate 0 closed: repo private (or verifiably clean if republished) and the IP memo exists
A full-history sweep for origin-deployment identifiers returns zero matches
CI green with the demand engine and cascade math under test; tsc clean
Auth + tenancy demoable with two isolated orgs
/idea-fit re-run at day 90 produces BUILD ON or a deliberate, documented decision to keep it internal

techStack.suggested: Keep the current stack: React 18 + TypeScript + Vite, Express, Drizzle ORM + Postgres, Radix/shadcn UI
techStack.rationale: The stack is fine and the product works; the debt is hygiene, not architecture. A fit report never proposes a rewrite — it proposes deleting the boilerplate that came with the app's origin and building the missing quality floor inside what exists.

buildKit.buildPrompt:
You are working in FleetSync, an existing operator-side frac sand logistics planner — React 18 + TypeScript + Vite frontend (Radix/shadcn), Express backend, Drizzle ORM on Postgres. It is a working ~20K-LOC product born as an internal tool; this is a brownfield hardening engagement, not a greenfield build. Do not rewrite, do not restyle, do not add product surface.

Context you must respect: this repository had an internal-tool origin, and repository hygiene work (history rewrite, data-file removal) is being handled separately by the owner — never commit data exports, seed data with real-looking operational content, or any company-identifiable strings. If you encounter files like planning exports or attached internal documents, flag them to the owner; do not copy their contents anywhere.

What already works and must not regress: the frac-job Gantt with drag-and-drop and lane cascading; the Excel-like daily allocation grid with inline/bulk editing, drag-to-fill, and its three summary footer rows (hauler totals, frac needs, surplus); real-time conflict detection; scenario management with sandbox isolation; the truck-demand calculation engine with date-segmented overrides; the NPT journal; CSV/XLSX import and export.

Build, in this order:
1. Delete server/replit_integrations entirely (dead chat/image scaffolding from the app's origin; it accounts for 13 of the 15 current tsc errors). Rename the package from 'rest-express' to 'fleetsync'. Get `npx tsc` fully clean, including the two real errors in client/src/pages/workload.tsx (startDate/endDate accessed on a type that lacks them — trace whether the schema or the page is wrong before patching).
2. Stand up Vitest with a test script in package.json. First suites, in priority order: the truck-demand engine (stages/day × tons/stage → loads/day, storage-type and override paths), allocation grid totals (hauler totals, frac needs, surplus math), and lane-cascading date arithmetic. These calculations allocate real trucks against real downtime costs — they are the money math and currently have zero coverage.
3. Add a CI workflow that gates on typecheck + tests.
4. Replace the PLANNER_USERNAMES env-var access control with real authentication and per-organization tenancy (all existing entities scoped to an org; two seeded demo orgs proving isolation). Keep the planner/viewer role distinction.
5. Add an audit trail: who changed which schedule/allocation/scenario and when, queryable per frac job.

Do NOT build: dispatch or telematics features, billing, mobile apps, marketing/landing surface, or any integration that assumes one company's data shapes. Keep all seed/demo data obviously fictional (generic pad names like 'Pad A-01', generic hauler names).

Start with step 1 and stop after `npx tsc` is clean and the boilerplate is gone — show me the diff before touching auth or tests.

--- BUSINESS CONTEXT ---
Product: hardware-neutral, operator-side frac sand logistics planning (Gantt + daily truck-allocation grid + scenarios). Wedge: completions/sand-logistics coordinators at gas-weighted shale operators running multiple frac fleets — a plan-time buyer the incumbents structurally ignore.
Scores: Opportunity 6/10 (narrow but real-budget buyer set); Problem 8/10 (a crew consumes a truckload every ~10 minutes 24/7; last-mile is up to one-third of delivered sand cost; waiting-on-sand runs $5,500-$10,000+/hr); Feasibility 7/10 (built and production-shaped, held down by the missing quality floor this engagement fixes); Why-now 7/10 (2026-27 LNG wave: record EIA gas-output projections, Appalachia needs ~50 more rigs/frac crews, ~824 DUCs queued, sand intensity ~2,500 lb/lateral-ft).
Market gap: every incumbent's software is welded to their hardware or services — PropX/PropConnect (Liberty) to containers, Solaris to silos, Atlas Opti to its own Permian sand-and-trucking loop, Vorto to dispatch marketplaces. The operator's neutral cross-fleet planning layer defaults to Excel. FleetSync sells the plan itself.
Current verdict from the fit report: PARK the product ambition pending two non-market gates (public-repo data hygiene, in progress; written IP/employment-conflict clarity) — which is why this engagement builds trust infrastructure (tests, auth, audit trail) and explicitly not customer-facing surface. If the gates clear, per-fleet SaaS at $1,500-$4,000/fleet/mo (est.) priced against $10K/hr downtime is the model, entered via free instrumented pilots.
Key risks to respect: quality trust (the demand engine allocates expensive resources — test it), data hygiene (nothing company-identifiable ever enters the repo), and neutrality (no vendor- or company-specific assumptions in core logic).
Sources and full research: ideas/frac-sand-logistics-planner/idea.json in the Idea Lab library; estimates marked (est.).
